old_claim.note: Pigment Creation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No pigment-creation edge remains because the former Blombos citation duplicated that pigment node and did not establish a historical lineage into the first- or second-century Pseudo-Democritean alchemical corpus.

source_shape.source_locator: Chapter title and summary date the treatise to the first or second century CE and describe varied recipes and procedures without tracing them to Blombos ochre processing.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The scoped alchemy record is a Greco-Egyptian recipe text, not a duplicate label for prehistoric pigment-compound production.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Similarity in mixing colored materials across 100,000 years cannot establish direct transmission or technical dependence between the two records.

ontology_before: The Blombos pigment workshop was duplicated under a broad alchemy label and then made its own prerequisite.
ontology_after: Pigment production and the much later source-pinned alchemical recipe corpus remain distinct technologies without an invented lineage.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: early_alchemy_material_science no longer directly depends on pigment_creation.
would_reject_if: Historical evidence established direct transmission from the Blombos pigment practice into the Pseudo-Democritean recipe tradition.
short_reason: Prehistoric pigment processing is not evidence for a direct alchemical-text lineage.
